meta.originalTitle: The Art of Deception 欺骗的艺术

children.text: You should spend about 20 minutes on Questions 27–40, which are based on Reading Passage 3 below.
Do tiny changes of facial expression show whether someone is telling lies?

Forty years ago, the research psychologist Dr Paul Ekman was addressing a group of young psychiatrists in training when he was asked a question, the answer to which has kept him busy ever since. Suppose the group wanted to know whether a particular patient who swears they are telling the truth really is. They look and sound sincere. So here is the question: is there any way you can be sure they are telling the truth? Ekman did not know the answer then, but he wanted to find out.

As part of his research, he had already filmed a series of 12-minute interviews with psychiatric patients. In a subsequent conversation, one of the patients told him that she had lied to him. So Ekman sat and looked at the film but saw nothing noteworthy. Then he slowed it down and looked again. Then he slowed it down even further. And suddenly, there, across just two frames of the film, he saw it: an intense expression of extreme anguish. It lasted less than a fifteenth of a second, but once he had spotted the first expression, he soon found three more examples in that same interview. He termed his discovery "micro-expressions”: very rapid, intense demonstrations of emotion that the subject intended to conceal.

Over the course of the next four decades, Ekman successfully demonstrated a proposition first suggested by Charles Darwin: that the ways in which we express rage, disgust, contempt, fear, surprise, happiness and sadness are universal. The facial muscles triggered by those seven basic emotions are, he has shown, essentially standard, regardless of language and culture, from the US to Japan and Brazil to Papua New Guinea. What is more, expressions of emotion are impossible to suppress and, particularly when we are lying, micro-expressions of powerfully felt emotions will inevitably flit across our face before we get the chance to stop them.

Fortunately for liars, most people will fail to spot these fleeting signals of inner torment. Of the 15,000 Ekman has tested, only 50 people, whom he calls “naturals”, have been able to do it. But given a little more training, Ekman says, almost anyone can develop the skill. He should know: since these tests were completed in the mid-1980s and the first publication of his research, he has been called in by the FBI and CIA (among countless more law-enforcement and other agencies around the world), not just to solve cases, but to teach them how to use his technique for themselves. He has held workshops for defence and prosecution lawyers, health professionals, even jealous spouses, all of them wanting to know exactly when someone is not being 100 percent candid.

Most recently, Ekman's research has resulted in a new television series about the exploits of the fictional Dr Cal Lightman, a scientist who studies involuntary body language to discover not only if you are lying, but why you might have been motivated to do so. According to the publicity blurb, Lightman is a human lie detector, even more accurate than a polygraph test. Ekman concedes he was sceptical when the producer first approached him with the idea of turning his life's work into a TV series, and initially would have stopped the project if he could. In particular, he was fearful that the show would exaggerate the effectiveness of his techniques and create the quite inaccurate impression among audiences that criminals could no longer hope to get away with lying. In the worst-case scenario, he was concerned about unfair convictions—that one day someone not properly trained in his techniques might be sitting on a jury and wrongly find someone guilty of a crime simply on the basis of a television programme.

In the end, though, he was won over because the series is unusual in several respects. It is the first time, as far as Ekman is aware, that a commercial TV drama has been based on the work of just one scientist. That scientist is also deeply involved in the project, talking through plot ideas and checking five successive drafts of each script to ensure details are correct. He was also impressed with the producer's manifestly serious and well-intentioned reasons for making the programme. Now that the first series has been completed, he believes probably 80–90 per cent of the show is based on fact and that's good enough for what it is. After all, it is a drama, not a documentary.

Ekman, incidentally, professes to have been a terrible liar ever since he was a small boy and observes that the ability to detect a lie and the ability to lie successfully are completely unrelated. He has been asked by people running for high office if he could teach them to become more credible with the public but has always refused to use his skills in that way on ethical grounds. He also insists that there are various kinds of lies. A “true” lie can be identified by having two essential characteristics: there must be a deliberate intent to mislead and there must be no notification that this is what is occurring. This means that an actor or a poker player isn't a true liar. They are supposed to deceive you—it's part of the game-and the same is true of flattery. He prefers to focus on the kinds of lies where the liar would be in grave trouble if they were found out, and where the target would feel properly aggrieved if they knew.
According to the writer, Ekman became interested in lying after a question from his
The writer refers to the 12-minute interviews in order to
A illustrate how frequently patients lie.
B describe the origins of Ekman's theories.
C compare Ekman's research to previous studies.
D show how patients' behaviour is affected by filming.
What is the writer's point in the third paragraph?
A Micro-expressions are common to all people.
B Recent research has refuted an old idea.
C With practice we can learn to control our micro-expressions.
D Human society is too complex to allow for generalisations.
What are we told about Ekman's conclusions from his tests?
A It's natural for people to lie.
B Few untrained people can detect lying.
C Most liars suffer from periods of depression.
D All of his subjects were trained to identify micro-expressions.
What point does the writer make about Ekman's techniques in the fourth paragraph?
A They take a decade to teach.
B They have been in great demand.
C They have aroused the suspicions of some agencies.
D They can be used by a limited range of occupations.

explanationSection.text:
答案：C（students，学生）
解析：定位 Paragraph 1 中 “the research psychologist Dr Paul Ekman was addressing a group of young psychiatrists in training when he was asked a question”，“年轻实习精神科医师” 是他的授课对象，即 “学生”，因此选 C。A（同行）、B（患者）、D（教师）均与原文矛盾。
答案：B（describe the origins of Ekman’s theories. 描述埃克曼理论的起源）
解析：Paragraph 2 通过 “观看访谈录像发现微表情” 的过程，阐述埃克曼 “微表情理论” 的诞生，核心是 “理论起源”，因此选 B。A（说明患者说谎频率）、C（将其研究与前人对比）、D（展示录像对患者行为的影响）均偏离目的。
答案：A（Micro-expressions are common to all people. 微表情对所有人都通用）
解析：定位 Paragraph 3 中 “the ways in which we express rage, disgust... are universal... regardless of language and culture”，“普遍性” 对应 “common to all people”，因此选 A。B（近期研究反驳旧观点）、C（通过练习可控制微表情）、D（人类社会复杂无法概括）均与原文矛盾。
答案：B（Few untrained people can detect lying. 极少未经训练者能识别谎言）
解析：定位 Paragraph 4 中 “Of the 15 000 Ekman has tested, only 50 people, whom he calls ‘naturals’, have been able to do it. But given a little more training, Ekman says, almost anyone can develop the skill”，“仅 50 人天生能识别，需训练才能掌握” 对应 “few untrained people can detect lying”，因此选 B。A（说谎是人类本能）、C（多数说谎者有抑郁期）、D（所有受试者均接受过识别微表情的训练）均未提及。
答案：B（They have been in great demand. 需求很大）
解析：定位 Paragraph 4 中 “he has been called in by the FBI and CIA (among countless more law-enforcement and other agencies around the world)... He has held workshops for defence and prosecution lawyers, health professionals, even jealous spouses”，“全球多领域机构邀请、举办多类工作坊” 体现 “需求大”，因此选 B。A（需十年才能掌握）、C（引发部分机构怀疑）、D（仅少数职业可使用）均与原文矛盾。

items.text:
（1）题目 27：According to the writer, Ekman became interested in lying after a question from his（作者认为，埃克曼对说谎产生兴趣是源于谁的问题？）
答案：C（students，学生）
解析：定位 Paragraph 1 中 “the research psychologist Dr Paul Ekman was addressing a group of young psychiatrists in training when he was asked a question”，“年轻实习精神科医师” 是他的授课对象，即 “学生”，因此选 C。A（同行）、B（患者）、D（教师）均与原文矛盾。

（2）题目 28：The writer refers to the 12-minute interviews in order to（作者提及 12 分钟访谈的目的是？）
答案：B（describe the origins of Ekman’s theories. 描述埃克曼理论的起源）
解析：Paragraph 2 通过 “观看访谈录像发现微表情” 的过程，阐述埃克曼 “微表情理论” 的诞生，核心是 “理论起源”，因此选 B。A（说明患者说谎频率）、C（将其研究与前人对比）、D（展示录像对患者行为的影响）均偏离目的。

（3）题目 29：What is the writer’s point in the third paragraph?（作者在第三段的观点是？）
答案：A（Micro-expressions are common to all people. 微表情对所有人都通用）
解析：定位 Paragraph 3 中 “the ways in which we express rage, disgust... are universal... regardless of language and culture”，“普遍性” 对应 “common to all people”，因此选 A。B（近期研究反驳旧观点）、C（通过练习可控制微表情）、D（人类社会复杂无法概括）均与原文矛盾。

（4）题目 30：What are we told about Ekman’s conclusions from his tests?（关于埃克曼的测试结论，文中提到什么？）
答案：B（Few untrained people can detect lying. 极少未经训练者能识别谎言）
解析：定位 Paragraph 4 中 “Of the 15 000 Ekman has tested, only 50 people, whom he calls ‘naturals’, have been able to do it. But given a little more training, Ekman says, almost anyone can develop the skill”，“仅 50 人天生能识别，需训练才能掌握” 对应 “few untrained people can detect lying”，因此选 B。A（说谎是人类本能）、C（多数说谎者有抑郁期）、D（所有受试者均接受过识别微表情的训练）均未提及。

（5）题目 31：What point does the writer make about Ekman’s techniques in the fourth paragraph?（作者在第四段中关于埃克曼的技巧有何观点？）
答案：B（They have been in great demand. 需求很大）
解析：定位 Paragraph 4 中 “he has been called in by the FBI and CIA (among countless more law-enforcement and other agencies around the world)... He has held workshops for defence and prosecution lawyers, health professionals, even jealous spouses”，“全球多领域机构邀请、举办多类工作坊” 体现 “需求大”，因此选 B。A（需十年才能掌握）、C（引发部分机构怀疑）、D（仅少数职业可使用）均与原文矛盾。

children.text: Complete the summary using the list of words,
The television series based on Ekman's work

A new TV series based on Ekman's work features a hero named Lightman, who detects lies. Initially, Ekman was unenthusiastic about the TV project because he feared the possibility of encouraging viewers'
. For example, he was worried that one day the programme could result in
not being carried out. Ultimately, though, he has given the show his blessing because he is not aware of any other comparable programme based on a single person's
of the show's producer have been another pleasant surprise considering the genre of the programme. Ekman is happy with the show's overall

explanationSection.text:
答案：C（false beliefs，错误观念）
解析：定位 Paragraph 5 中 “feared that the show would exaggerate the effectiveness of his techniques and create the quite inaccurate impression among audiences”，“不准确印象” 对应 “false beliefs”，因此选 C。
答案：E（justice，正义）
解析：定位 Paragraph 5 中 “he was concerned about unfair convictions-that one day someone... might wrongly find someone guilty of a crime”，“不公定罪” 意味着 “正义无法实施”，因此选 E。
答案：H（research，研究）
解析：定位 Paragraph 6 中 “it is the first time... that a commercial TV drama has been based on the work of just one scientist”，“科学家的工作” 即 “research”，因此选 H。
答案：D（motives，动机）
解析：定位 Paragraph 6 中 “He was also impressed with the producer’s manifestly serious and well-intentioned reasons for making the programme”，“严肃善意的原因” 即 “motives”，因此选 D。
答案：F（accuracy，准确性）
解析：定位 Paragraph 6 中 “he believes probably 80–90 per cent of the show is based on fact and that’s good enough”，“基于事实的比例” 对应 “accuracy”，因此选 F。

items.text:
（1）题目 32：Initially, Ekman was unenthusiastic about the TV project because he feared the possibility of encouraging viewers’ .（起初埃克曼对电视剧项目不热衷，因担心助长观众的。）
答案：C（false beliefs，错误观念）
解析：定位 Paragraph 5 中 “feared that the show would exaggerate the effectiveness of his techniques and create the quite inaccurate impression among audiences”，“不准确印象” 对应 “false beliefs”，因此选 C。

（2）题目 33：For example, he was worried that one day the programme could result in \___\___ not being carried out.（例如，他担心该剧某天可能导致_\___\__无法实施。）
答案：E（justice，正义）
解析：定位 Paragraph 5 中 “he was concerned about unfair convictions-that one day someone... might wrongly find someone guilty of a crime”，“不公定罪” 意味着 “正义无法实施”，因此选 E。

（3）题目 34：Ultimately, though, he has given the show his blessing because he is not aware of any other comparable programme based on a single person’s .（但最终他认可该剧，因未发现其他类似节目基于单个人的。）
答案：H（research，研究）
解析：定位 Paragraph 6 中 “it is the first time... that a commercial TV drama has been based on the work of just one scientist”，“科学家的工作” 即 “research”，因此选 H。

（4）题目 35：The \___\___ of the show’s producer have been another pleasant surprise considering the genre of the programme.（考虑到剧集类型，制片人的_\___\__是另一个惊喜。）
答案：D（motives，动机）
解析：定位 Paragraph 6 中 “He was also impressed with the producer’s manifestly serious and well-intentioned reasons for making the programme”，“严肃善意的原因” 即 “motives”，因此选 D。

（5）题目 36：Ekman is happy with the show’s overall \___\___.（埃克曼对该剧的整体_\___\__满意。）
答案：F（accuracy，准确性）
解析：定位 Paragraph 6 中 “he believes probably 80–90 per cent of the show is based on fact and that’s good enough”，“基于事实的比例” 对应 “accuracy”，因此选 F。

children.text: Do the following statements agree with the claims of the writer in Reading Passage 3?
In boxes 37–40 on your answer sheet, write
if the statement agrees with the claims of the writer
if the statement contradicts the claims of the writer
if it is impossible to say what the writer thinks about this
Ekman regrets the lies he told as a child.
People who are good at lying tend to be good at detecting lies.
Ekman has worked with poker players to help them lie more convincingly.
Ekman is more interested in the types of lies with serious consequences.

explanationSection.text:
答案：NOT GIVEN（未提及）
解析：Paragraph 7 仅提到 “he professes to have been a terrible liar ever since he was a small boy”，“不擅长说谎”，未提及 “后悔”，无相关信息，因此判定为 NOT GIVEN。
答案：NO（错误）
解析：定位 Paragraph 7 中 “the ability to detect a lie and the ability to lie successfully are completely unrelated”，“完全无关” 与题干 “往往擅长” 描述矛盾，因此判定为 NO。
答案：NOT GIVEN（未提及）
解析：Paragraph 7 仅提到 “a poker player isn’t a true liar. They are supposed to deceive you-it’s part of the game”，“扑克玩家不算真正说谎者”，未提及 “合作帮助他们说谎”，无相关信息，因此判定为 NOT GIVEN。
答案：YES（正确）
解析：定位 Paragraph 7 中 “He prefers to focus on the kinds of lies where the liar would be in grave trouble if they were found out, and where the target would feel properly aggrieved if they knew”，“关注被揭穿会导致麻烦的谎言” 即 “有严重后果的谎言”，与题干描述一致，因此判定为 YES。

items.text:
（1）题目 37：Ekman regrets the lies he told as a child.（埃克曼后悔儿时说过的谎）
答案：NOT GIVEN（未提及）
解析：Paragraph 7 仅提到 “he professes to have been a terrible liar ever since he was a small boy”，“不擅长说谎”，未提及 “后悔”，无相关信息，因此判定为 NOT GIVEN。

（2）题目 38：People who are good at lying tend to be good at detecting lies.（擅长说谎的人往往擅长识别谎言）
答案：NO（错误）
解析：定位 Paragraph 7 中 “the ability to detect a lie and the ability to lie successfully are completely unrelated”，“完全无关” 与题干 “往往擅长” 描述矛盾，因此判定为 NO。

（3）题目 39：Ekman has worked with poker players to help them lie more convincingly.（埃克曼联手扑克玩家，帮助他们更可信地说谎）
答案：NOT GIVEN（未提及）
解析：Paragraph 7 仅提到 “a poker player isn’t a true liar. They are supposed to deceive you-it’s part of the game”，“扑克玩家不算真正说谎者”，未提及 “合作帮助他们说谎”，无相关信息，因此判定为 NOT GIVEN。

（4）题目 40：Ekman is more interested in the types of lies with serious consequences.（埃克曼对有严重后果的谎言类型更感兴趣）
答案：YES（正确）
解析：定位 Paragraph 7 中 “He prefers to focus on the kinds of lies where the liar would be in grave trouble if they were found out, and where the target would feel properly aggrieved if they knew”，“关注被揭穿会导致麻烦的谎言” 即 “有严重后果的谎言”，与题干描述一致，因此判定为 YES。
